# PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial {#sec-ch13}

This part of the course provides students with the opportunity to work
with their own experimental data. The selected experimental paradigm is
postural control. Briefly, postural sway data will be collected under
different standing conditions and subsequently used as a benchmark for
applying the statistical methods presented later in the course.

As discussed in @sec-stabilometry, stabilometry is considered the gold
standard technique for assessing postural sway. Stabilometric analysis
is based on changes in the position of the body's center of pressure
(CoP), which are estimated from ground reaction forces.

Collecting stabilometric data during class would be impractical,
particularly for a large group of students. Therefore, taking advantage
of the widespread availability of smartphones, this course adopts a
smartphone-based approach for acquiring postural sway data.

Although students are free to use any application they consider
appropriate, all practical activities in this course will rely on the
PhyPhox initiative.

::: {.callout-note title="This chapter ..."}
*... provides a brief tutorial on using PhyPhox to collect postural sway
data. Step-by-step instructions are provided for installing the app,
running the SmartPosture experiment, and saving and exporting the
collected data.*
:::

\

## What is PhyPhox?

[PhyPhox](https://phyphox.org/) is an educational application developed
at RWTH Aachen University, Germany. It was designed to enable students
to perform scientific experiments using their smartphones, effectively
transforming the device into a portable physics laboratory.

> **PhyPhox** stands for *physical phone experiments*.

With PhyPhox, it is possible to investigate a wide variety of physical
phenomena, including:

-   *energy dissipated during collisions;*
-   *centripetal acceleration;*
-   *the acceleration due to gravity;*
-   *the frequency and period of a spring oscillator;*
-   *among many other applications.*

PhyPhox operates through
[**experiments**](https://phyphox.org/experiments/), which correspond to
predefined sets of instructions governing data acquisition. Users may
either select one of the built-in experiments or create custom
experiments using the PhyPhox
[web editor](https://phyphox.org/editor/).

\

## PhyPhox and Postural Sways

For the purposes of this course, a custom PhyPhox experiment---named the
**SmartPosture Experiment**---was developed specifically for measuring
postural sway.

The **SmartPosture** experiment provides an indirect estimate of
postural sway based on signals acquired from three smartphone sensors:
the accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer. Body sway is estimated
from changes in smartphone orientation about its $x$ and $y$ axes.
According to the reference system illustrated in @fig-1-3-1, the
following convention applies:

-   Sway in the sagittal plane is estimated from smartphone rotation
    about the $x$ axis. Thus, *pitch* reflects the
    antero-posterior displacement ($y_{cog}$) of the body's center of
    gravity (CoG).

-   Sway in the frontal plane is estimated from smartphone rotation
    about the $y$ axis. Accordingly, *roll* reflects the
    medio-lateral displacement ($x_{cog}$) of the body CoG.

![Reference system for measuring postural
sways](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/1-3-1_smartphone-reference-system.png){#fig-1-3-1
width="60%"}

More specifically, smartphone rotations about the $x$ and $y$ axes are
estimated using the orientation *virtual sensor*^[*signals from the physical sensors are combined using sensor-fusion techniques to estimate device orientation, effectively emulating a dedicated orientation sensor*].

Rotations in the sagittal plane are represented by $\theta$ (*pitch*),
whereas rotations in the frontal plane are represented by $\gamma$
(*roll*). The coordinates of the body center of gravity (CoG) projected
onto the horizontal plane are therefore given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{cog}(t) &= \gamma(t) \times h \\
y_{cog}(t) &= -\theta(t) \times h 
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-cog-from-smartphone}

The negative sign ensures that a counterclockwise rotation about the
$x$ axis corresponds to a forward body sway. The parameter $h$
represents the height of the body CoG above the ground (see
@fig-1-2-2), estimated from the anthropometric data reported by
[Winter](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/book/10.1002/9780470549148)
@winter09:

$$
h \approx 0.55 \times b_\text{h}
$$

where $b_\text{h}$ denotes total body height.

::: {.callout-note title="Beware of the assumptions"}
*Two important assumptions underlie the use of
@eq-cog-from-smartphone to estimate postural sway from smartphone
orientation.*

*First, body rotations are assumed to be sufficiently small for the
approximation* $sin \theta \approx \theta$ *to remain valid.*

*Second, the smartphone must be held in portrait orientation, with its
$z$ axis aligned with the direction of gravity (see @fig-1-3-2).*
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Validity of the assumptions"}
*During quiet standing, body movements are typically small enough for
both assumptions to be considered valid.*

*For the educational purposes of this course, the postural sway data
estimated from @eq-cog-from-smartphone provide an appropriate framework
for learning the processing of stabilometric data and statistical analysis.*
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
![How to hold the
smartphone.](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/1-3-2_holding-smartphone.png){#fig-1-3-2
width="95%"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
During quiet standing, the arms should hang naturally alongside the
body, while the head should remain in a neutral position (neither
flexed nor extended).

Although the posture illustrated in @fig-1-3-2 does not exactly
correspond to that adopted during a standard stabilometric assessment,
it is presented solely to facilitate data acquisition without requiring
assistance from another person.

*Hold the smartphone firmly and avoid moving any body segment
immediately after starting data acquisition* (see the experimental
protocol below).
:::
:::::

\

## Installing PhyPhox and the SmartPosture Experiment

To download the PhyPhox app, use the appropriate link below according
to the operating system installed on your smartphone.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
[![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/phyphox-android.png){height="3em"}](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=de.rwth_aachen.phyphox&utm_source=global_co&utm_medium=prtnr&utm_content=Mar2515&utm_campaign=PartBadge&pcampaignid=MKT-Other-global-all-co-prtnr-py-PartBadge-Mar2515-1)
:::
::: {.column width="10%"}
:::
::: {.column width="30%"}
[![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/phyphox-ios.png){height="3em"}](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/phyphox/id1127319693?ls=1)
:::
:::::

The next step is to import the **SmartPosture** experiment into
PhyPhox. To do so, follow the four steps illustrated in @fig-1-3-3 and
described below.

![Steps for importing the SmartPosture experiment into the PhyPhox
collection.](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/1-3-3_load-experiment.png){#fig-1-3-3
width="100%"}

\

:::::::::: panel-tabset

### Step 1: Run

#### Launch the PhyPhox App

When you launch PhyPhox, the home screen displays a list of the
built-in experiments available in the application. Because
**SmartPosture** is a custom experiment, it must first be imported.
Press the **$+$** icon located in the upper-right corner of the screen
(see panel 1 in @fig-1-3-3).

### Step 2: Add

#### Import the SmartPosture Experiment

::::: columns
::: {.column width="70%"}

PhyPhox provides three different methods for importing experiments, all
illustrated in panel 2 of @fig-1-3-3. The simplest approach is to scan
the QR code shown in panel 3 and reproduced here for convenience.

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/qrcode.png)
:::
:::::

### Step 3: Save

#### Save the Experiment to Your Collection

After scanning the QR code, PhyPhox will automatically download and load
the **SmartPosture** experiment.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/save-to-collection.png)
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="66%"}

Because this is the first time the experiment has been loaded, PhyPhox
will display a prompt asking whether the remotely hosted experiment
should be added to your collection. Select **Save to collection** so
that the experiment becomes permanently available from the home screen.

:::
::::::

::::::::::

After the **SmartPosture** experiment has been successfully added to
your collection, it will appear in the PhyPhox home screen and will be
ready for use.

\

## Running the SmartPosture Experiment

This section introduces the basic features of the **SmartPosture** experiment and explains how to use the application to collect postural sway data.

\

### Launching SmartPosture

After completing the installation procedure described in the previous
section, the **SmartPosture** experiment will appear in your PhyPhox
experiment collection.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/smartposture-in-collection.png)
:::

::: {.column width="3%"}
:::

::: {.column width="57%"}
Launch PhyPhox and open the **SmartPosture** experiment. It should be
listed under the
[**Biomedical**]{style="background-color:#38A2FF; color: white"} tab on
the PhyPhox home screen, as illustrated in the figure.
:::
::::::

\

### SmartPosture Interface

The **SmartPosture** experiment comprises two main screens, each
providing a different representation of the stabilometric data (see
@fig-1-2-6). These screens are illustrated in @fig-1-3-4.

![The two screens of the SmartPosture
experiment.](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/1-3-4_smart-posture-screens.png){#fig-1-3-4
width="80%"}

##### **Graphs**

The *Stabilogram* screen displays two time-series graphs. The upper
graph shows CoG displacement in the medio-lateral (frontal plane)
direction, whereas the lower graph displays CoG displacement in the
antero-posterior (sagittal plane) direction.

The *Statokinesigram* screen displays a single graph showing the
trajectory of the body CoG projected onto the horizontal plane.

##### **Slider**

The slider available on both screens should be adjusted according to the
participant's height, expressed in centimeters. This value is used to
scale the smartphone rotations, thereby estimating the horizontal
projection of the body CoG according to
@eq-cog-from-smartphone.

##### **Dropdown Menus**

Two dropdown menus allow the user to specify the base of support and the
standing condition adopted during the stabilometric trial. Each menu
provides three available options.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/base-of-support.png)

The **base of support** is defined according to foot position during the
trial.

-   **wide**: feet positioned approximately hip-width apart.
-   **narrow**: feet positioned close together.
-   **tandem**: one foot positioned directly in front of the other.
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/standing-condition.png)

The **standing condition** specifies the experimental condition under
which the trial is performed.

-   **eyes open**: standing quietly with the eyes open.
-   **eyes closed**: standing quietly with the eyes closed.
-   **still**: standing while deliberately minimizing postural sway.
:::
::::::

##### **Icon Buttons**

Three icon buttons are available in the upper-right corner of the
screen.

-   ![](/include/images/icons/phyphox-play.png){height="1.5em"} **Play**:
    starts and stops data acquisition. When pressed, the play icon
    changes to a pause icon while data are being recorded.

-   ![](/include/images/icons/phyphox-bin.png){height="1.5em"} **Clear**:
    removes all CoG samples currently stored in memory.

-   ![](/include/images/icons/phyphox-settings.png){height="1.5em"} **Settings**:
    opens the configuration menu for the current PhyPhox experiment.

\

##### **Exporting Data** {#sec-phyphox-data-export}

After completing a stabilometric trial with the **SmartPosture**
experiment, the recorded CoG data can be exported and stored locally on
your smartphone. The export procedure is illustrated in @fig-1-3-5.

After stopping data acquisition, press the **Settings** button. A menu
will appear containing several options, including **Export Data**.
Select this option to open the export menu. Among the available export
formats, choose **Excel**, and then press **Export Data** in the
bottom-right corner of the screen. PhyPhox will then prompt you to
choose where the exported file should be saved, either locally on the
device or in a cloud storage service.

![Exporting CoG data in
SmartPosture.](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/1-3-5_export-data.png){#fig-1-3-5
width="100%"}

\

## The Experimental Protocol {#sec-exp-protocol}

After installing PhyPhox and importing the **SmartPosture** experiment
into your PhyPhox collection, collect postural sway data according to
the protocol below.

1. Position yourself as illustrated in @fig-1-3-2 while holding the
   smartphone.

2. Collect postural sway data using the **SmartPosture** interface by
   following the sequence of steps (**a** to **h**) described below and
   illustrated in the accompanying video (@fig-1-3-6).

3. Repeat the procedure for each combination of base of support
   (*wide*, *narrow*, and *tandem*) and standing condition (*eyes
   open*, *eyes closed*, and *still*).

:::{.boxed}
::::: columns

::: {.column width="40%"}

:::{.small-text}

**a**: Launch the **SmartPosture** experiment.

**b**: Select either the *Statokinesigram* or *Stabilogram*
visualization.

**c**: Set your height.

**d**: Select the appropriate base of support.

**e**: Select the standing condition.

**f**: Assume the required posture and press the **Play**
button.

**g**: Maintain the standing position for 40 s under the selected base
of support and standing condition.

**h**: Export the data in **Excel** format **before performing any other
action**.

:::
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
![Steps for collecting postural sway data with
SmartPosture.](/include/images/part_1/ch_3/smartposture-tutorial.mp4){#fig-1-3-6
width="100%"}
:::

:::::
:::

:::{.callout-important title="What's next?"}

*At the end of the experiment, you should have nine Excel files
corresponding to the nine combinations of base of support (3) and
standing condition (3).*

*Upload all nine files to the Moodle platform. Instructions for the
submission procedure will be provided during class.*
:::
